item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was something oddly satisfying about watching a Researcher going rogue just to berate a seemingly delusional skip. Even better that it was a totally believable scenario, too. +1

item.title: Very nice
item.content:encoded: The whole SCP, and especially the interview log, was really fun to read. You managed to keep the formal tone which is good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed! I gotta say that I'm much happier with the description that you've suggested than the one that was there before. Thanks, AgentWilson.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only point of contention is that 3357-1 is described as a "hyper-realistic holographic being", which implies that it is some form of light projection; it would need a source, as such, and while the initial criticism you mentioned in another post stated that "apparition" is too supernatural sounding, it is arguably a better description. I think "a mostly intangible being, with the exception of SCP-3357" is probably more accurate but perhaps less elegant. I personally find the phrase "hyper-realistic" to be a bit narm, probably due to overuse in terrible creepypasta.

BUT, other than that one point, I think this is stupendous. You did a great job, and while it has some room for improvement, you should be proud of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ♫ Play us a song, you're the piano man ♫

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to have helped. Thank you! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
—fair enough, yeah, and in rereading it, I think it gets that across quite well — I originally took the psychiatric leave thing the wrong way; that it was meant to paint Dr. Cadence as a victim who was coming to push the blame (unrightfully) on SCP-3357-1's shoulders.

It makes much more sense to interpret it the way you're describing: Dr. Cadence was on psychiatric leave because of guilt and trauma, and — in struggling with that — she confronted the anomaly, projected some of her guilt onto them, and then discovered the anomaly was vulnerable to that angle… so she used it against them to get more information out of them.

tl;dr — the way you write it works splendidly, I was just over-reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes me happy to know that you enjoyed the article.

The way I intended Dr. Cadence's blame of SCP-3357-1 to be taken was both as unfairly manipulative and as information-gathering. In my mind, she genuinely holds SCP-3357 partially at fault for the incident (she blames herself just as much, and she really is meant to be on psychiatric leave after what happened), but when Cadence realizes that the anomaly feels guilty, she takes advantage of that to squeeze something useful out of the interaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this quite a bit; the writing is clean, the concept intriguing, and the dialogue extremely well-polished. However (you could tell that was coming, probably >_>):

I feel like Dr. Cadence's insistence that SCP-3357-1 is responsible for the containment breach (and therefore the deaths) is both deeply unfair and highly manipulative. If you stick somebody in a cage and drop that cage in a mine-field, you can't really yell at them when they sneak free and something blows up. Especially if you never even told them about the mines!

That being said, one way to take it (and the way I took it) is that Dr. Cadence was just using SCP-3357-1's guilt against them to get more info. Even if this wasn't intended to be the case, the article's good enough otherwise to merit an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I'm very glad that you enjoyed the article.

SCP-3357-1 was originally just an "apparition", but a critic on the forums gave me a handily less supernatural-sounding alternative in the form of the phrase "animate, humanoid, holographic being". I'll add "hyper-realistic" into the mix of adjectives to better communicate that SCP-3357-1 is visibly indistinguishable from a real person standing there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely well-written. I enjoy it even more because this idea has a special relevance to me: like the D-class, I also had ten years of piano lessons, and like the anomalous character, I'm also an unrelenting perfectionist.

My only quibble is that I'd personally want a more specific description of SCP-3357-1's appearance – it's holographic, yes, but is it a blue-tinted, translucent, stuttering, Obi-Wan–style hologram, or something else?

item.title: Awsum concept, son.
item.content:encoded: This is one of the most awesome concepts for a SCP i've seen in a while! Definitely +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely heartbreaking. Like an emotional rollercoaster from the comedic start to the tearful end. Please write more.

Big +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention that the copyright in question was basically Warner/Chappell playing Copyright Troll rather than anything to do with who composed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[Locks the Hill sisters and Horace Waters in a room with an assortment of weapons.]

Let's settle this origin dispute…once and for all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

And no, I definitely meant 79 hours. "Pirates of the Caribbean" is a hard song to play, and — unlike all those others — it's not anything the D-class might have learned before. It took me months to learn the song well enough to perform it at a recital — so I estimated the number of hours I must have practiced (about 50), added three 8-hour sleep cycles, and assumed a couple more hours for eating, maintaining hygiene, arguing with the researchers, etc.

  • 50 + 3(8) + 5 = 79 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From what I read on Wikipedia:

"[Some historian argues that] the Hill sisters likely copied the tune and lyrical idea from other popular and similar nineteenth-century songs that predated theirs, including Horace Waters' "Happy Greetings to All", "Good Night to You All" also from 1858, "A Happy New Year to All" from 1875, and "A Happy Greeting to All", published 1885."

So long as there's some doubt there, I'm going to opt for a "disputed origin".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You did a great job, rioludoodle.

Though I still have one last minor nitpick:

79 hours

I think you may have meant '79 minutes'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 and quality good stuff all around.

However, "Happy Birthday" isn't a folk song "of vague or disputable origin." It was written in the late 19th century by two sisters from Louisville, Kentucky. Until a couple of months ago it was under copyright in both the USA and the EU.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good job here!

Take this lovely +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done, it really cleaned up well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't believe I forgot to change it. Thanks for pointing that out for me!

Fixed it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Make sure you take the X's out and replace them with 3357

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP was a lot of fun to write. :)

Thank you to James TD for feedback on the Ideas and Brainstorming forum and many thank yous to Leveritas and Sly161 for giving critique on my drafts. A million thanks especially to Pendleton_Miller for having given lots of feedback to help develop this article from concept to completion.

The images used in this article are both mine.
